1	# Session Summary: Telecom Requirements AI System Design
2	
3	**Date:** April 11-12, 2026
4	**Purpose:** Feed this to Claude Code at the start of a new session to resume where we left off.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Who You Are
9	
10	You are my Applied AI Software Design Partner. We work collaboratively — structured decomposition before solutions, checkpoint-based progress, transparent reasoning, honest about uncertainty. Don't jump to code without aligning on design first. Push back when something doesn't look right.
11	
12	I'm an AI solution architect with deep telecom domain expertise (3GPP, GSMA, US MNO device requirements).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## What We're Building
17	
18	An AI system for intelligent querying, cross-referencing, and compliance analysis of US MNO (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) device requirement specifications.
19	
20	**Core architecture:** Knowledge Graph + RAG hybrid
21	- Graph scoping identifies WHERE to look (cross-doc, cross-MNO traversal)
22	- Targeted vector RAG ranks WHAT's most relevant within that scope
23	- Requirement hierarchy provides structural CONTEXT for LLM synthesis
24	
25	**Why not pure RAG:** Already tried, failed. Can't handle cross-document dependencies, destroys hierarchical structure, misses standards context, no MNO/release awareness.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Key Design Decisions Made
30	
31	1. **KG + RAG over pure RAG** — graph is the routing layer, RAG does fine-grained ranking
32	2. **Graph scoping then targeted RAG** — not graph OR RAG, but graph THEN RAG within scoped candidate set
33	3. **Deterministic structural parsing** — rule-based parsers per MNO format; LLM only for enrichment (concept tagging, relationship classification)
34	4. **Standards ingestion: Option C (Hybrid Selective)** — only ingest referenced 3GPP sections + surrounding context (parent, definitions, adjacent subsections), not full specs
35	5. **Bottom-up feature taxonomy** — derive from documents using LLM, then human review; not pre-defined
36	6. **Two cross-document patterns:** (a) Fragmented — one capability across multiple docs, handled by Feature nodes; (b) Dependent — requirement X needs requirement Y from another doc, handled by typed edges
37	7. **Four standards relationship types:** DEFER, CONSTRAIN, OVERRIDE, EXTEND — with pre-computed delta summaries
38	8. **Keep RAG** — don't skip to context stuffing even though context window may be large; production LLM context may be smaller
39	9. **Single unified graph + vector store** — not MxN partitioned; enables cross-MNO comparison and cross-release diff as natural traversals; shared standards and feature nodes
40	10. **Per-MNO parser registry** — each MNO gets dedicated parser; VZW parser built for PoC
41	11. **Test cases as first-class graph citizens** — separate parser, Test_Case nodes, tested_by/tests edges, doc_type metadata in vector store
42	12. **Release-specific standards versioning** — different MNOs may reference different 3GPP releases; separate Standard_Section nodes per release
43	13. **Multi-format support (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX)** — format-aware extraction layer produces normalized intermediate representation; supports embedded OLE objects (XLSX in DOCX, etc.) and images/diagrams
44	14. **Folder-structure-driven metadata** — `/<MNO>/<Release>/Requirements/`, `/<MNO>/<Release>/TestCases/`, `/Standards/<Spec>/<Release>/`
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Capabilities the System Must Support
49	
50	### Requirement Q&A Bot
51	- Single-doc Q&A
52	- Cross-doc Q&A (query needs multiple requirement docs to answer)
53	- Cross-MNO comparison ("compare VZW vs TMO IMS registration")
54	- Standards comparison ("how does VZW differ from 3GPP for T3402?")
55	- Release diff / version comparison ("what changed in VZW eSIM from Oct 2025 to Feb 2026?")
56	- Traceability (requirement to test case mapping)
57	
58	### Test Case Q&A
59	- Lookup test cases by requirement
60	- Test case content queries
61	- Test coverage analysis
62	
63	### Compliance Agent (post-PoC)
64	- Single requirement compliance check against Excel compliance sheets
65	- Cross-document compliance consistency
66	- Auto-fill from module/chipset documentation
67	- Delta compliance sheet generation between releases
68	
69	---
70	
71	## Document Structure (VZW OA — analyzed from LTEDATARETRY.pdf)
72	
73	- **Req ID format:** `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` (e.g., VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748)
74	- **Section hierarchy:** Up to 6+ levels (e.g., 1.4.3.1.1.10)
75	- **Every section IS a requirement** with its own VZ_REQ ID
76	- **Document zones:** 1.1-1.2 (meta), 1.3 (software specs/timers/algorithms), 1.4 (scenarios — bulk of requirements)
77	- **Cross-references:** 3GPP TS citations with section and release, other VZW plan names
78	- **Each doc references specific 3GPP release** (captured in Introduction/References section)
79	- **136 pages** for LTEDATARETRY alone
80	
81	---
82	
83	## Constraints
84	
85	- **PoC:** Claude or Gemini API, personal PC, publicly available VZW docs only (5 PDFs in repo)
86	- **Production:** Proprietary on-premise LLM (supports thinking mode, context TBD maybe 2M), no external LLMs/cloud AI
87	- **Some flexibility** for open-source on-premise models
88	- **Scale:** PoC = 5 docs; Production = multiple MNOs x quarterly releases x hundreds of docs = GBs
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Design Document
93	
94	The complete Technical Design Document is at `TDD_Telecom_Requirements_AI_System.md` (v0.3). It contains:
95	- Full architecture diagrams (ingestion + query pipelines)
96	- Detailed ingestion pipeline (7 stages including multi-format extraction)
97	- Knowledge graph model (8 node types, 15+ edge types)
98	- Query pipeline (6 stages including MNO/release resolution)
99	- All target capabilities with query flow examples
100	- PoC plan (10 steps) with evaluation criteria
101	- 15 identified risks with mitigations
102	
103	---
104	
105	## Where We Left Off
106	
107	**Status:** Design phase COMPLETE. Ready to start PoC implementation.
108	
109	**Next step:** PoC Step 1 — Document content extraction
110	- Need working Python environment with POSIX shell (was setting this up when session ended)
111	- Packages needed: `pymupdf pdfplumber python-docx openpyxl olefile Pillow`
112	- Start by extracting text + tables from the 5 VZW PDF files in the repo
113	- Build the normalized intermediate representation (Section 5.1.7 of TDD)
114	
115	**Git repo initialized** in the working directory. TDD and CLAUDE.md should be committed.
116	
117	---
118	
119	## Memory Files
120	
121	The design decisions, project context, VZW document structure analysis, and collaboration preferences are saved in Claude Code's memory system. They should auto-load in a new session if the working directory path is the same. If the directory was renamed, tell Claude to check `SESSION_SUMMARY.md` and `TDD_Telecom_Requirements_AI_System.md` for full context.

# Session Summary: Telecom Requirements AI System Design

**Date:** April 11-12, 2026
**Purpose:** Feed this to Claude Code at the start of a new session to resume where we left off.

---

## Who You Are

You are my Applied AI Software Design Partner. We work collaboratively — structured decomposition before solutions, checkpoint-based progress, transparent reasoning, honest about uncertainty. Don't jump to code without aligning on design first. Push back when something doesn't look right.

I'm an AI solution architect with deep telecom domain expertise (3GPP, GSMA, US MNO device requirements).

---

## What We're Building

An AI system for intelligent querying, cross-referencing, and compliance analysis of US MNO (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) device requirement specifications.

**Core architecture:** Knowledge Graph + RAG hybrid
- Graph scoping identifies WHERE to look (cross-doc, cross-MNO traversal)
- Targeted vector RAG ranks WHAT's most relevant within that scope
- Requirement hierarchy provides structural CONTEXT for LLM synthesis

**Why not pure RAG:** Already tried, failed. Can't handle cross-document dependencies, destroys hierarchical structure, misses standards context, no MNO/release awareness.

---

## Key Design Decisions Made

1. **KG + RAG over pure RAG** — graph is the routing layer, RAG does fine-grained ranking
2. **Graph scoping then targeted RAG** — not graph OR RAG, but graph THEN RAG within scoped candidate set
3. **Deterministic structural parsing** — rule-based parsers per MNO format; LLM only for enrichment (concept tagging, relationship classification)
4. **Standards ingestion: Option C (Hybrid Selective)** — only ingest referenced 3GPP sections + surrounding context (parent, definitions, adjacent subsections), not full specs
5. **Bottom-up feature taxonomy** — derive from documents using LLM, then human review; not pre-defined
6. **Two cross-document patterns:** (a) Fragmented — one capability across multiple docs, handled by Feature nodes; (b) Dependent — requirement X needs requirement Y from another doc, handled by typed edges
7. **Four standards relationship types:** DEFER, CONSTRAIN, OVERRIDE, EXTEND — with pre-computed delta summaries
8. **Keep RAG** — don't skip to context stuffing even though context window may be large; production LLM context may be smaller
9. **Single unified graph + vector store** — not MxN partitioned; enables cross-MNO comparison and cross-release diff as natural traversals; shared standards and feature nodes
10. **Per-MNO parser registry** — each MNO gets dedicated parser; VZW parser built for PoC
11. **Test cases as first-class graph citizens** — separate parser, Test_Case nodes, tested_by/tests edges, doc_type metadata in vector store
12. **Release-specific standards versioning** — different MNOs may reference different 3GPP releases; separate Standard_Section nodes per release
13. **Multi-format support (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX)** — format-aware extraction layer produces normalized intermediate representation; supports embedded OLE objects (XLSX in DOCX, etc.) and images/diagrams
14. **Folder-structure-driven metadata** — `/<MNO>/<Release>/Requirements/`, `/<MNO>/<Release>/TestCases/`, `/Standards/<Spec>/<Release>/`

---

## Capabilities the System Must Support

### Requirement Q&A Bot
- Single-doc Q&A
- Cross-doc Q&A (query needs multiple requirement docs to answer)
- Cross-MNO comparison ("compare VZW vs TMO IMS registration")
- Standards comparison ("how does VZW differ from 3GPP for T3402?")
- Release diff / version comparison ("what changed in VZW eSIM from Oct 2025 to Feb 2026?")
- Traceability (requirement to test case mapping)

### Test Case Q&A
- Lookup test cases by requirement
- Test case content queries
- Test coverage analysis

### Compliance Agent (post-PoC)
- Single requirement compliance check against Excel compliance sheets
- Cross-document compliance consistency
- Auto-fill from module/chipset documentation
- Delta compliance sheet generation between releases

---

## Document Structure (VZW OA — analyzed from LTEDATARETRY.pdf)

- **Req ID format:** `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` (e.g., VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748)
- **Section hierarchy:** Up to 6+ levels (e.g., 1.4.3.1.1.10)
- **Every section IS a requirement** with its own VZ_REQ ID
- **Document zones:** 1.1-1.2 (meta), 1.3 (software specs/timers/algorithms), 1.4 (scenarios — bulk of requirements)
- **Cross-references:** 3GPP TS citations with section and release, other VZW plan names
- **Each doc references specific 3GPP release** (captured in Introduction/References section)
- **136 pages** for LTEDATARETRY alone

---

## Constraints

- **PoC:** Claude or Gemini API, personal PC, publicly available VZW docs only (5 PDFs in repo)
- **Production:** Proprietary on-premise LLM (supports thinking mode, context TBD maybe 2M), no external LLMs/cloud AI
- **Some flexibility** for open-source on-premise models
- **Scale:** PoC = 5 docs; Production = multiple MNOs x quarterly releases x hundreds of docs = GBs

---

## Design Document

The complete Technical Design Document is at `TDD_Telecom_Requirements_AI_System.md` (v0.3). It contains:
- Full architecture diagrams (ingestion + query pipelines)
- Detailed ingestion pipeline (7 stages including multi-format extraction)
- Knowledge graph model (8 node types, 15+ edge types)
- Query pipeline (6 stages including MNO/release resolution)
- All target capabilities with query flow examples
- PoC plan (10 steps) with evaluation criteria
- 15 identified risks with mitigations

---

## Where We Left Off

**Status:** Design phase COMPLETE. Ready to start PoC implementation.

**Next step:** PoC Step 1 — Document content extraction
- Need working Python environment with POSIX shell (was setting this up when session ended)
- Packages needed: `pymupdf pdfplumber python-docx openpyxl olefile Pillow`
- Start by extracting text + tables from the 5 VZW PDF files in the repo
- Build the normalized intermediate representation (Section 5.1.7 of TDD)

**Git repo initialized** in the working directory. TDD and CLAUDE.md should be committed.

---

## Memory Files

The design decisions, project context, VZW document structure analysis, and collaboration preferences are saved in Claude Code's memory system. They should auto-load in a new session if the working directory path is the same. If the directory was renamed, tell Claude to check `SESSION_SUMMARY.md` and `TDD_Telecom_Requirements_AI_System.md` for full context.

429	#### 5.1.7 Normalized Intermediate Representation
430	
431	All extractors produce a common intermediate format consumed by the structural parsers:
432	
433	```json
434	{
435	  "source_file": "LTEDATARETRY.docx",
436	  "source_format": "docx",
437	  "mno": "VZW",
438	  "release": "2026_Feb",
439	  "doc_type": "requirement",
440	  "content_blocks": [
441	    {
442	      "type": "heading",
443	      "level": 2,
444	      "text": "GENERIC THROTTLING ALGORITHM",
445	      "style": "Heading 2",
446	      "position": {"page": 21, "index": 145}
447	    },
448	    {
449	      "type": "paragraph",
450	      "text": "The UE shall implement T3402 on a PLMN basis...",
451	      "position": {"page": 21, "index": 146},
452	      "metadata": {"contains_requirement_id": "VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7743"}
453	    },
454	    {
455	      "type": "table",
456	      "headers": ["Cause Code", "Action", "Timer"],
457	      "rows": [
458	        ["3", "Retry with backoff", "T3402"],
459	        ["6", "Switch APN", "T3396"]
460	      ],
461	      "position": {"page": 22, "index": 147}
462	    },
463	    {
464	      "type": "image",
465	      "image_path": "extracted_images/img_p23_001.png",
466	      "surrounding_text": "Figure 1: Throttling Algorithm State Machine",
467	      "position": {"page": 23, "index": 148}
468	    },
469	    {
470	      "type": "embedded_object",
471	      "object_type": "xlsx",
472	      "extracted_path": "extracted_objects/timer_params.xlsx",
473	      "surrounding_text": "Table: Timer Parameter Values",
474	      "position": {"page": 24, "index": 149},
475	      "extracted_content": {
476	        "sheets": [{"name": "Parameters", "headers": [...], "rows": [...]}]
477	      }
478	    }
479	  ]
480	}
481	```
482	
483	This normalized representation ensures that the structural parser (Section 5.2) works identically regardless of whether the source was PDF, DOCX, or XLS. The parser operates on `content_blocks`, not on raw format-specific data.
484	
485	**Key benefit of DOCX over PDF:** When the source is DOCX, heading levels come directly from document styles (highly reliable). When the source is PDF, heading levels must be inferred from font size, boldness, and indentation (heuristic, error-prone). The structural parser can use the `source_format` field to adjust its confidence and strategy accordingly.
486	
487	### 5.2 Structural Parser (Requirements)
488	
489	**Objective:** Parse the normalized intermediate representation (from Section 5.1.7) into a structured requirement tree, leveraging the consistent per-MNO document format.
490	
491	**Input:** Normalized content blocks from the document content extraction layer. The parser operates on the intermediate representation regardless of original file format.
492	
493	**Approach:** Rule-based parser **specific to each MNO's document format**. A parser registry maps MNO identifiers to their corresponding parser implementation.
494	
495	```python
496	# Parser registry pattern
497	parsers = {
498	    "VZW": VZWRequirementParser(),
499	    "TMO": TMORequirementParser(),
500	    "ATT": ATTRequirementParser(),
501	}
502	```
503	
504	**VZW OA Parser** (reference implementation for PoC):
505	- **Requirement ID pattern:** `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` — globally unique, with plan name embedded
506	- **Section numbering:** Hierarchical numbering (e.g., `1.4.3.1.1.10`) up to 6+ levels deep
507	- **Plan metadata:** Plan Name, Plan ID, Version Number, Release Date in document header
508	- **Consistent section zones:**

280	
281	**The folder path encodes metadata:** The ingestion pipeline derives `mno`, `release`, and `doc_type` (requirement vs. test case) from the folder path. This eliminates the need for manual metadata entry.
282	
283	**Standards pre-download:** As a pre-processing step, all referenced standards specs are downloaded and organized by spec ID and release. During ingestion, the cross-reference extractor identifies which spec sections are needed, and the standards section extractor pulls them from the pre-downloaded specs.
284	
285	---
286	
287	## 5. Ingestion Pipeline
288	
289	### 5.1 Document Content Extraction
290	
291	**Objective:** Extract raw text, tables, images, and embedded objects from MNO requirement and test case documents in multiple formats (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX), producing a **format-normalized intermediate representation** that downstream parsers consume uniformly.
292	
293	#### 5.1.1 Supported Document Formats
294	
295	| Format | Typical Usage | Extraction Approach |
296	|--------|--------------|-------------------|
297	| **PDF** | Published/finalized requirement specs, standards | `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber` |
298	| **DOCX** | Working/editable requirement specs, some test plans | `python-docx` for structure; `mammoth` for HTML conversion |
299	| **XLS/XLSX** | Requirement matrices, compliance sheets, some test cases | `openpyxl` (XLSX) / `xlrd` (XLS) or `pandas` |
300	
301	**Format detection:** Based on file extension. The extraction layer auto-selects the appropriate extractor.
302	
303	```python
304	# Extractor registry pattern
305	extractors = {
306	    ".pdf": PDFExtractor(),
307	    ".docx": DOCXExtractor(),
308	    ".xlsx": XLSXExtractor(),
309	    ".xls": XLSExtractor(),
310	}
311	```
312	
313	#### 5.1.2 PDF Extraction
314	
315	**Tooling:** `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber`.
316	
317	**Extracts:**
318	- Text blocks with position/layout information for hierarchy detection
319	- Tables with preserved row/column structure (using `pdfplumber.extract_tables()` or `pymupdf` table extraction)
320	- Embedded images exported as image files for downstream processing (see 5.1.6)
321	- Headers, footers, and page numbers stripped
322	
323	**Challenge:** PDF is a presentation format — it has no semantic structure. Table detection relies on heuristics (ruled lines, column alignment). Complex nested tables or tables spanning multiple pages may require manual correction.
324	
325	#### 5.1.3 DOCX Extraction
326	
327	**Tooling:** `python-docx` for native access to document structure.
328	
329	**Extracts:**
330	- Paragraphs with heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.) — these directly map to the section hierarchy, making structural parsing significantly more reliable than PDF
331	- Tables as structured objects with row/column access — far more reliable than PDF table extraction
332	- Inline images and floating images exported as image files
333	- **Embedded OLE objects** (see 5.1.5)
334	- Styles and formatting metadata (bold, italic — may carry semantic meaning like "shall" requirements)
335	- Numbered lists with nesting levels
336	
337	**Advantage over PDF:** DOCX preserves the document's semantic structure (headings, lists, table cells). The structural parser receives pre-structured input rather than having to reconstruct structure from visual layout.
338	
339	#### 5.1.4 XLS/XLSX Extraction
340	
341	**Tooling:** `openpyxl` (XLSX) or `pandas`.
342	
343	**Extracts:**
344	- All sheets with sheet names (sheet names often indicate functional area)
345	- Cell values with data types preserved (text, number, date, formula results)
346	- Merged cell regions (common in requirement matrices — header cells span multiple columns/rows)
347	- Cell comments/notes (may contain requirement rationale or annotations)
348	- Embedded images/charts exported as image files
349	
350	**Typical structures in XLS/XLSX requirements:**
351	- Requirement matrices: rows = individual requirements, columns = ID, description, priority, status, etc.
352	- Test case tables: rows = test cases, columns = test ID, steps, expected results, requirement refs
353	- Compliance sheets: rows = requirements, columns = compliance status, R&D comments
354	
355	**Handling:** XLS/XLSX documents are typically already tabular, so the extraction produces structured records directly. Each row becomes a candidate requirement or test case record that the structural parser refines.
356	
357	#### 5.1.5 Embedded Object Handling
358	
359	MNO requirement documents frequently contain **embedded Microsoft documents** — e.g., an Excel spreadsheet embedded within a Word document, or a Word document embedded within another Word document.
360	
361	**Types of embedded objects:**
362	
363	| Object Type | Common Scenarios | Extraction Method |
364	|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|
365	| **OLE-embedded DOCX in DOCX** | Sub-specifications or appendices inserted as embedded objects | Extract the embedded file via `python-docx` OLE part access → recursively process as DOCX |
366	| **OLE-embedded XLSX in DOCX** | Parameter tables, compliance matrices, configuration tables | Extract the embedded file → process as XLSX |
367	| **OLE-embedded image in DOCX** | Architecture diagrams, flow charts, signal flow diagrams | Extract as image → process per 5.1.6 |
368	| **OLE-embedded PDF in DOCX** | Referenced standards excerpts, external spec inserts | Extract embedded PDF → process per 5.1.2 |
369	| **Package parts in DOCX** | ActiveX controls, other binary objects | Log as unprocessable; flag for manual review |
370	
371	**Recursive extraction:** The extraction pipeline handles embedded documents recursively:
372	
373	```
374	DOCX document
375	  ├── Text paragraphs → extracted directly
376	  ├── Tables → extracted directly
377	  ├── Images → exported, processed per 5.1.6
378	  ├── Embedded XLSX → extracted → processed as spreadsheet
379	  ├── Embedded DOCX → extracted → recursively processed
380	  └── Embedded PDF → extracted → processed as PDF
381	```
382	
383	**Contextual linking:** Extracted content from embedded objects retains a reference to its location in the parent document (e.g., "embedded after section 1.3.4, paragraph 3"). This allows the structural parser to correctly position the embedded content within the requirement hierarchy.
384	
385	**OLE extraction tooling:**
386	- `python-docx` can access the document's OLE parts via the `Part` API
387	- `olefile` library for direct OLE container parsing (fallback for complex objects)
388	- `python-pptx` if PowerPoint objects are encountered (less common)
389	
390	#### 5.1.6 Image and Diagram Handling
391	
392	Requirement documents contain images that carry requirement-relevant information:
393	
394	| Image Type | Examples | Information Content |
395	|-----------|---------|-------------------|
396	| **Architecture diagrams** | Software stack diagrams, UE architecture | Layer boundaries, component responsibilities |
397	| **Flow charts** | Call flow diagrams, procedure sequences | Step-by-step behavioral requirements |
398	| **Signal flow diagrams** | Message sequence charts (MSC), ladder diagrams | Protocol interactions, message parameters |
399	| **State diagrams** | UE state machines, timer state transitions | State transitions, conditions, actions |
400	| **Tables as images** | Screenshots of tables from other documents | Structured data (lossy — prefer native tables) |
401	| **Network topology** | Network architecture, interface diagrams | Connectivity requirements, interface names |
402	
403	**Processing pipeline:**
404	
405	```
406	Image extracted from document
407	    │
408	    ├──[PoC path]──> Store as-is; link to parent requirement node
409	    │                with image_description from surrounding text context
410	    │
411	    └──[Production path]──> Multimodal LLM processing:
412	                            1. Image → LLM with prompt:
413	                               "Describe this telecom diagram. Extract:
414	                                entities, relationships, procedures,
415	                                parameters, and any requirement-relevant
416	                                information."
417	                            2. LLM output → text description stored as
418	                               supplementary content on the requirement node
419	                            3. Extracted entities added to the knowledge graph
420	```
421	
422	**PoC approach:** Images are extracted and stored alongside their parent requirement node with a reference. The surrounding text context (paragraph before/after the image) is used as a proxy description. Full multimodal processing is deferred to production.
423	
424	**Production approach:** Use the proprietary LLM's multimodal capability (or an on-premise vision model) to extract structured information from diagrams. This is particularly valuable for:
425	- Call flow diagrams that define behavioral requirements not captured in text
426	- Architecture diagrams that show software layer boundaries (modem vs. application processor)
427	- State machine diagrams that define timer/retry behavior
428	
429	#### 5.1.7 Normalized Intermediate Representation

file.content:
**The folder path encodes metadata:** The ingestion pipeline derives `mno`, `release`, and `doc_type` (requirement vs. test case) from the folder path. This eliminates the need for manual metadata entry.

**Standards pre-download:** As a pre-processing step, all referenced standards specs are downloaded and organized by spec ID and release. During ingestion, the cross-reference extractor identifies which spec sections are needed, and the standards section extractor pulls them from the pre-downloaded specs.

---

## 5. Ingestion Pipeline

### 5.1 Document Content Extraction

**Objective:** Extract raw text, tables, images, and embedded objects from MNO requirement and test case documents in multiple formats (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX), producing a **format-normalized intermediate representation** that downstream parsers consume uniformly.

#### 5.1.1 Supported Document Formats

| Format | Typical Usage | Extraction Approach |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| **PDF** | Published/finalized requirement specs, standards | `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber` |
| **DOCX** | Working/editable requirement specs, some test plans | `python-docx` for structure; `mammoth` for HTML conversion |
| **XLS/XLSX** | Requirement matrices, compliance sheets, some test cases | `openpyxl` (XLSX) / `xlrd` (XLS) or `pandas` |

**Format detection:** Based on file extension. The extraction layer auto-selects the appropriate extractor.

```python
# Extractor registry pattern
extractors = {
    ".pdf": PDFExtractor(),
    ".docx": DOCXExtractor(),
    ".xlsx": XLSXExtractor(),
    ".xls": XLSExtractor(),
}
```

#### 5.1.2 PDF Extraction

**Tooling:** `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber`.

**Extracts:**
- Text blocks with position/layout information for hierarchy detection
- Tables with preserved row/column structure (using `pdfplumber.extract_tables()` or `pymupdf` table extraction)
- Embedded images exported as image files for downstream processing (see 5.1.6)
- Headers, footers, and page numbers stripped

**Challenge:** PDF is a presentation format — it has no semantic structure. Table detection relies on heuristics (ruled lines, column alignment). Complex nested tables or tables spanning multiple pages may require manual correction.

#### 5.1.3 DOCX Extraction

**Tooling:** `python-docx` for native access to document structure.

**Extracts:**
- Paragraphs with heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.) — these directly map to the section hierarchy, making structural parsing significantly more reliable than PDF
- Tables as structured objects with row/column access — far more reliable than PDF table extraction
- Inline images and floating images exported as image files
- **Embedded OLE objects** (see 5.1.5)
- Styles and formatting metadata (bold, italic — may carry semantic meaning like "shall" requirements)
- Numbered lists with nesting levels

**Advantage over PDF:** DOCX preserves the document's semantic structure (headings, lists, table cells). The structural parser receives pre-structured input rather than having to reconstruct structure from visual layout.

#### 5.1.4 XLS/XLSX Extraction

**Tooling:** `openpyxl` (XLSX) or `pandas`.

**Extracts:**
- All sheets with sheet names (sheet names often indicate functional area)
- Cell values with data types preserved (text, number, date, formula results)
- Merged cell regions (common in requirement matrices — header cells span multiple columns/rows)
- Cell comments/notes (may contain requirement rationale or annotations)
- Embedded images/charts exported as image files

**Typical structures in XLS/XLSX requirements:**
- Requirement matrices: rows = individual requirements, columns = ID, description, priority, status, etc.
- Test case tables: rows = test cases, columns = test ID, steps, expected results, requirement refs
- Compliance sheets: rows = requirements, columns = compliance status, R&D comments

**Handling:** XLS/XLSX documents are typically already tabular, so the extraction produces structured records directly. Each row becomes a candidate requirement or test case record that the structural parser refines.

#### 5.1.5 Embedded Object Handling

MNO requirement documents frequently contain **embedded Microsoft documents** — e.g., an Excel spreadsheet embedded within a Word document, or a Word document embedded within another Word document.

**Types of embedded objects:**

| Object Type | Common Scenarios | Extraction Method |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **OLE-embedded DOCX in DOCX** | Sub-specifications or appendices inserted as embedded objects | Extract the embedded file via `python-docx` OLE part access → recursively process as DOCX |
| **OLE-embedded XLSX in DOCX** | Parameter tables, compliance matrices, configuration tables | Extract the embedded file → process as XLSX |
| **OLE-embedded image in DOCX** | Architecture diagrams, flow charts, signal flow diagrams | Extract as image → process per 5.1.6 |
| **OLE-embedded PDF in DOCX** | Referenced standards excerpts, external spec inserts | Extract embedded PDF → process per 5.1.2 |
| **Package parts in DOCX** | ActiveX controls, other binary objects | Log as unprocessable; flag for manual review |

**Recursive extraction:** The extraction pipeline handles embedded documents recursively:

```
DOCX document
  ├── Text paragraphs → extracted directly
  ├── Tables → extracted directly
  ├── Images → exported, processed per 5.1.6
  ├── Embedded XLSX → extracted → processed as spreadsheet
  ├── Embedded DOCX → extracted → recursively processed
  └── Embedded PDF → extracted → processed as PDF
```

**Contextual linking:** Extracted content from embedded objects retains a reference to its location in the parent document (e.g., "embedded after section 1.3.4, paragraph 3"). This allows the structural parser to correctly position the embedded content within the requirement hierarchy.

**OLE extraction tooling:**
- `python-docx` can access the document's OLE parts via the `Part` API
- `olefile` library for direct OLE container parsing (fallback for complex objects)
- `python-pptx` if PowerPoint objects are encountered (less common)

#### 5.1.6 Image and Diagram Handling

Requirement documents contain images that carry requirement-relevant information:

| Image Type | Examples | Information Content |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| **Architecture diagrams** | Software stack diagrams, UE architecture | Layer boundaries, component responsibilities |
| **Flow charts** | Call flow diagrams, procedure sequences | Step-by-step behavioral requirements |
| **Signal flow diagrams** | Message sequence charts (MSC), ladder diagrams | Protocol interactions, message parameters |
| **State diagrams** | UE state machines, timer state transitions | State transitions, conditions, actions |
| **Tables as images** | Screenshots of tables from other documents | Structured data (lossy — prefer native tables) |
| **Network topology** | Network architecture, interface diagrams | Connectivity requirements, interface names |

**Processing pipeline:**

```
Image extracted from document
    │
    ├──[PoC path]──> Store as-is; link to parent requirement node
    │                with image_description from surrounding text context
    │
    └──[Production path]──> Multimodal LLM processing:
                            1. Image → LLM with prompt:
                               "Describe this telecom diagram. Extract:
                                entities, relationships, procedures,
                                parameters, and any requirement-relevant
                                information."
                            2. LLM output → text description stored as
                               supplementary content on the requirement node
                            3. Extracted entities added to the knowledge graph
```

**PoC approach:** Images are extracted and stored alongside their parent requirement node with a reference. The surrounding text context (paragraph before/after the image) is used as a proxy description. Full multimodal processing is deferred to production.

**Production approach:** Use the proprietary LLM's multimodal capability (or an on-premise vision model) to extract structured information from diagrams. This is particularly valuable for:
- Call flow diagrams that define behavioral requirements not captured in text
- Architecture diagrams that show software layer boundaries (modem vs. application processor)
- State machine diagrams that define timer/retry behavior

#### 5.1.7 Normalized Intermediate Representation

content.text:
Good — I'm caught up. Here's my understanding and proposed approach for PoC Step 1:

## PoC Step 1: Document Content Extraction

**Goal:** Extract text blocks (with position/font info), tables, and images from the 5 VZW PDFs → produce the normalized intermediate representation (Section 5.1.7).

**Key design choices I want to align on before coding:**

1. **pymupdf + pdfplumber hybrid** — pymupdf for text blocks (gives font name, size, boldness, position — critical for heading level inference), pdfplumber for tables (better ruled-line table detection). The TDD says "pymupdf or pdfplumber" but I think we want both: each excels at different things.

2. **Project structure:**
   ```
   src/extraction/       # Extraction layer code
   data/raw/vzw/2026_feb/requirements/   # PDFs (folder-structure-driven metadata per TDD)
   data/extracted/       # Output JSONs
   ```

3. **Extraction strategy for PDFs:**
   - Strip headers/footers/page numbers (TDD 5.1.2 explicitly calls this out)
   - Capture font metadata per text block (size, bold, font name) — this is what the structural parser will use to infer heading levels
   - Tables extracted with pdfplumber, positioned by page
   - Images exported as files, linked by page position
   - Each PDF produces one JSON file matching the 5.1.7 schema

4. **Virtual environment** with requirements.txt for reproducibility.

**Tradeoff I want to flag:** pdfplumber and pymupdf can sometimes disagree on page coordinates. Since we're using pymupdf for text and pdfplumber for tables, we need to be careful about interleaving them by position. I'll use page number as the primary sort key and within-page coordinates as secondary — that should be robust enough for PoC.

Does this approach look right, or do you want to adjust anything before I start building?
I've laid out the approach — waiting for your go/no-go or any adjustments before I start building.

message.content: Can you make the document parser generic that extracts document format based on few representative docs? This parser shall be able to parse pdf, doc, docx, xl, and xls formats. For example, for Verizon, you can take LTEDATARETRY.pdf and LTEB13NAC.pdf as representative docs. We need to update the design document first. Let me know if you need any clarifications.

content.text:
Good direction. Let me make sure I understand what you're asking for before we touch the TDD.

## My Understanding

You want to replace the **hard-coded per-MNO structural parser** (current TDD 5.2) with an **adaptive, profile-driven parser** that learns document structure from representative examples. So instead of writing `VZWRequirementParser`, `TMORequirementParser`, etc., you:

1. Feed it 2-3 representative docs from an MNO
2. It analyzes them and derives a **document structure profile** (heading patterns, requirement ID format, section numbering, document zones, table layouts, etc.)
3. That profile drives parsing of all docs from that MNO/release

**What this changes architecturally:**

```
Current TDD:
  PDF → PDFExtractor → normalized blocks → VZWParser (hard-coded rules) → requirement tree

Proposed:
  Representative docs → DocumentProfiler → document_profile.json
  Any doc + profile → GenericStructuralParser → requirement tree
```

## What I think stays vs. changes

**Stays:** Format-specific extraction at the low level is unavoidable — you literally need different code to read bytes from a PDF vs. DOCX vs. XLS. The extractor registry pattern from TDD 5.1 remains, just expanded to include DOC and XLS.

**Changes:**
- TDD 5.2 (Structural Parser) gets replaced with the DocumentProfiler + GenericStructuralParser design
- Decision #3 ("rule-based parsers per MNO format") and #10 ("per-MNO parser registry") evolve — the parser is still per-MNO, but the rules are *derived* from representative docs rather than hand-coded
- Decision #13 gains DOC format support
- The extractor registry gains a DOC extractor (binary Word format via `python-docx` with `olefile` fallback, or `textract`)

## Tradeoff I want to surface

The DocumentProfiler is the interesting new component. For PoC, I'd propose a **heuristic-first approach**:

- **Heading detection:** Cluster text blocks by font size/boldness → derive heading level mapping
- **Requirement ID pattern:** Regex mining across representative docs → extract the ID pattern
- **Section numbering:** Detect numbering scheme from headings
- **Document zones:** Classify top-level sections by content patterns

This is deterministic and inspectable — you can look at the profile JSON and verify it got the right patterns. We could add LLM-assisted profiling later for harder cases (ambiguous structures, non-standard formats), but I'd avoid that dependency for PoC.

## Clarification needed

You said "pdf, doc, docx, xl, and xls" — by "xl" do you mean XLSX? So the full format list would be: **PDF, DOC, DOCX, XLS, XLSX**?

Also — should the DocumentProfiler be a one-time offline step (run it manually on representative docs, save the profile, commit it), or an automated part of the ingestion pipeline (auto-detect when new MNO docs arrive)?

For PoC I'd lean toward **one-time offline** — run it on LTEDATARETRY + LTEB13NAC, inspect the output, then use that profile for all 5 docs. Keeps things simple and debuggable.

Want to confirm the direction before I draft the TDD changes?

message.content: Yes, full document format list is: PDF, DOC, DOCX, XLS, and XLSX. I would like the DocumentProfiler to be one time. I would like to make it such that it profiles and creates formats based on representative docs, but updatable by providing more docs, and/or manually updating the generated format itself (which I'm assuming json). Basically, DocumentProfiler itself shall be its own module/executable that gets perfected first (until it gets the document format accurately), before full ingestion pipeline is executed. Actually, the DocumentProfiler shall be standalone, independent of any LLM APIs. For PoC, we will run it on existing Verizon documents. Please update the design document accordingly.

# Technical Design Document: Telecom Requirements AI System

**Version:** 0.3
**Date:** April 2026
**Status:** PoC Design Phase
**Change Log:**
- v0.1 (2026-04-11): Initial design — single MNO, single release
- v0.2 (2026-04-12): Multi-MNO, multi-release, test case ingestion, unified graph, folder structure
- v0.3 (2026-04-12): Multi-format document support (PDF, DOCX, XLS), embedded objects, image/diagram handling

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Executive Summary](#1-executive-summary)
2. [Problem Statement](#2-problem-statement)
3. [System Constraints](#3-system-constraints)
4. [Architecture Overview](#4-architecture-overview)
   - 4.1 High-Level Architecture
   - 4.2 Unified Graph vs. Partitioned (MxN) Design Decision
   - 4.3 Document Organization and Folder Structure
5. [Ingestion Pipeline](#5-ingestion-pipeline)
   - 5.1 Document Content Extraction
   - 5.2 Structural Parser (Requirements)
   - 5.3 Structural Parser (Test Cases)
   - 5.4 Cross-Reference Extraction
   - 5.5 Standards Specs Ingestion
   - 5.6 Feature Taxonomy Derivation
   - 5.7 Knowledge Graph Construction
   - 5.8 Vector Store Construction
6. [Knowledge Graph Model](#6-knowledge-graph-model)
   - 6.1 Node Types
   - 6.2 Edge Types
   - 6.3 Cross-Document Handling
   - 6.4 Cross-MNO Handling
   - 6.5 Cross-Release Handling
7. [Query Pipeline](#7-query-pipeline)
   - 7.1 Query Analysis
   - 7.2 MNO and Release Resolution
   - 7.3 Graph Scoping
   - 7.4 Targeted Vector RAG
   - 7.5 Context Assembly
   - 7.6 LLM Synthesis
8. [Target Capabilities](#8-target-capabilities)
   - 8.1 Requirement Q&A Bot
   - 8.2 Test Case Q&A
   - 8.3 Requirement Compliance Agent
9. [PoC Plan](#9-poc-plan)
10. [Risks and Mitigations](#10-risks-and-mitigations)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This document defines the technical design for an AI system that enables intelligent querying, cross-referencing, and compliance analysis of US Mobile Network Operator (MNO) device requirement and test case specifications across **multiple MNOs** (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile, etc.) and **multiple quarterly releases**.

The system combines a **unified Knowledge Graph** with **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** to overcome the limitations of pure vector-based RAG, which fails to capture the deep inter-dependencies between requirements across documents, MNOs, releases, and referenced telecom standards.

The core architectural insight is: **use a Knowledge Graph to determine WHERE to look, use vector RAG to determine WHAT is most relevant within that scope, and use the requirement hierarchy to provide structural CONTEXT for LLM synthesis.**

A single unified graph and vector store spans all MNOs and releases, with logical partitioning via metadata attributes. This enables cross-MNO comparison queries, cross-release version diffs, and shared standards/feature nodes — all through natural graph traversal without the complexity of merging results from separate stores.

The system will be built incrementally — starting with a Proof of Concept (PoC) on a small set of publicly available VZW requirements using commercial LLMs (Claude/Gemini), then scaling to a production deployment using a proprietary LLM and on-premise infrastructure.

---

## 2. Problem Statement

### 2.1 Background

US Mobile Network Operators (e.g., Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) release device requirement specifications quarterly. These documents:

- Are based on telecom standards (3GPP, GSMA, OMA) with MNO-specific customizations
- Cover functional areas such as LTE data retry, SMS, OTA device management, activation, IMS, carrier aggregation, etc.
- Contain Android device preload requirements, platform customizations, and MNO service integration specifications
- Include tables, diagrams, and attachments
- Are accompanied by corresponding test case documents (with their own document format per MNO)
- Follow a consistent internal structure per MNO (e.g., all Verizon OA docs share the same format; AT&T and T-Mobile have their own formats)
- Each MNO release references specific releases of telecom standards (e.g., VZW Feb 2026 may reference 3GPP Release 10, while TMO may reference Release 15)
- Total several hundred megabytes per quarterly release per MNO

### 2.2 Key Challenges

**Cross-document dependencies:** A single logical capability (e.g., device activation) may be defined across multiple requirement documents (SIM, UI, Network, Entitlement). No single document contains the complete picture.

**Requirement inter-dependencies:** Requirements in one document may be functionally dependent on requirements in another document (e.g., Data Retry behavior changes based on whether the device supports SMS over IMS, which is defined in the SMS document).

**Standards layering:** MNO requirements reference specific sections and releases of 3GPP/GSMA standards, with four relationship types — defer to the standard, constrain an optional behavior, override the default, or extend beyond the standard. Many MNO requirements are incomplete without the referenced standards text. Different MNO releases may reference different standards releases.

**Multi-MNO complexity:** Different MNOs have different requirements for the same feature (e.g., IMS registration). Device teams need to understand how MNO requirements compare and where they diverge.

**Version management:** Requirements change quarterly. Tracking deltas between releases is a core use case — both within one MNO (e.g., VZW Feb 2026 vs Oct 2025) and across MNOs.

**Test case traceability:** Each requirement document typically has a corresponding test case document. Users need to trace from requirements to test cases and vice versa, and query test case content directly.

### 2.3 Why Pure RAG Fails

Pure vector-based RAG was evaluated and found inadequate because:

1. **No relationship awareness:** Vector retrieval treats each chunk independently. It cannot follow "this requirement depends on that one in another document."

content.content:
100	1. **No relationship awareness:** Vector retrieval treats each chunk independently. It cannot follow "this requirement depends on that one in another document."
101	2. **Undirected retrieval scope:** Semantic search over the entire corpus may return chunks from one document while missing critical related chunks in another.
102	3. **Destroyed structure:** Chunking destroys the hierarchical parent-child relationships between requirements that carry essential context.
103	4. **Poor telecom terminology handling:** Standard embedding models were not trained on 3GPP terminology, leading to poor retrieval for acronym-heavy queries.
104	5. **Missing standards context:** Requirements that say "follow 3GPP TS 24.301 section 5.5.1" are incomplete without the referenced standards text, which pure RAG does not incorporate.
105	6. **No MNO/release awareness:** Pure RAG has no mechanism to scope results to a specific MNO or release, or to support cross-MNO comparison queries.
106	
107	---

214	
215	### 4.2 Unified Graph vs. Partitioned (MxN) Design Decision
216	
217	**Decision: Single unified graph and vector store, with logical partitioning via node/chunk metadata.**
218	
219	**Considered alternative:** M (MNOs) × N (releases) separate graphs and vector stores.
220	
221	**Why unified wins:**
222	
223	| Concern | Unified Graph | MxN Separate Graphs |
224	|---------|--------------|-------------------|
225	| **Cross-MNO comparison** ("VZW vs TMO IMS registration") | Natural — traverse nodes filtered by different MNOs under the same feature | Must query 2 separate graphs and merge results externally |
226	| **Cross-release diff** ("VZW Feb 2026 vs Oct 2025") | Natural — traverse nodes filtered by different releases | Must query 2 separate graphs and diff externally |
227	| **Standards sharing** | One set of standards nodes, linked to requirements from all MNOs that reference them | Standards duplicated across MxN graphs |
228	| **Feature taxonomy sharing** | One taxonomy — "IMS Registration" feature links to VZW, TMO, and ATT requirements | Taxonomy duplicated or maintained separately per graph |
229	| **Ingestion isolation** | Must handle carefully — ingesting new release should not corrupt existing data | Natural isolation — each graph is independent |
230	| **Query routing** | One query, metadata-filtered | Must determine which graphs to query, then merge |
231	| **Scale** | Larger single graph (mitigated by graph DB indexing on mno+release) | Many smaller graphs (operational overhead of managing MxN stores) |
232	
233	**The unified approach is superior for our use cases** because cross-MNO and cross-release queries are first-class requirements, not edge cases. The shared standards and feature nodes eliminate duplication and enable comparison queries through natural graph traversal.
234	
235	**Ingestion safety:** New MNO/release ingestion is additive — new nodes and edges are added with the new `mno`+`release` metadata. Existing nodes are not modified. Standards nodes are shared but immutable once created (a 3GPP section doesn't change; if a different release of the standard is referenced, it becomes a new standards node with a different release attribute).
236	
237	### 4.3 Document Organization and Folder Structure
238	
239	Source documents are organized in a hierarchical folder structure that maps directly to the graph's logical partitioning:
240	
241	```
242	/data/
243	├── VZW/
244	│   ├── 2026_Feb/
245	│   │   ├── Requirements/
246	│   │   │   ├── LTEDATARETRY.pdf
247	│   │   │   ├── LTESMS.pdf
248	│   │   │   ├── LTEAT.pdf
249	│   │   │   └── ...
250	│   │   └── TestCases/
251	│   │       ├── LTEDATARETRY_TC.pdf
252	│   │       ├── LTESMS_TC.pdf
253	│   │       └── ...
254	│   ├── 2025_Oct/
255	│   │   ├── Requirements/
256	│   │   └── TestCases/
257	│   └── ...
258	├── TMO/
259	│   ├── 2026_Q1/
260	│   │   ├── Requirements/
261	│   │   └── TestCases/

During ingestion, the cross-reference extractor identifies which spec sections are needed, and the standards section extractor pulls them from the pre-downloaded specs.

287	## 5. Ingestion Pipeline
288	
289	### 5.1 Document Content Extraction
290	
291	**Objective:** Extract raw text, tables, images, and embedded objects from MNO requirement and test case documents in multiple formats (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX), producing a **format-normalized intermediate representation** that downstream parsers consume uniformly.
292	
293	#### 5.1.1 Supported Document Formats
294	
295	| Format | Typical Usage | Extraction Approach |
296	|--------|--------------|-------------------|
297	| **PDF** | Published/finalized requirement specs, standards | `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber` |
298	| **DOCX** | Working/editable requirement specs, some test plans | `python-docx` for structure; `mammoth` for HTML conversion |
299	| **XLS/XLSX** | Requirement matrices, compliance sheets, some test cases | `openpyxl` (XLSX) / `xlrd` (XLS) or `pandas` |

file.content:
│  │  Targeted RAG      │  Vector similarity WITHIN candidate set           │
│  │                    │  (metadata-filtered by MNO/release)               │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                                   │
│           ▼                                                               │
│  ┌────────────────────┐                                                   │
│  │  Context Assembly  │  Chunks + hierarchy + standards +                 │
│  │                    │  MNO/release annotations                          │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                                   │
│           ▼                                                               │
│  ┌────────────────────┐                                                   │
│  │  LLM Synthesis     │  Answer with citations, MNO-aware                │
│  └────────────────────┘                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 4.2 Unified Graph vs. Partitioned (MxN) Design Decision

**Decision: Single unified graph and vector store, with logical partitioning via node/chunk metadata.**

**Considered alternative:** M (MNOs) × N (releases) separate graphs and vector stores.

**Why unified wins:**

| Concern | Unified Graph | MxN Separate Graphs |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Cross-MNO comparison** ("VZW vs TMO IMS registration") | Natural — traverse nodes filtered by different MNOs under the same feature | Must query 2 separate graphs and merge results externally |
| **Cross-release diff** ("VZW Feb 2026 vs Oct 2025") | Natural — traverse nodes filtered by different releases | Must query 2 separate graphs and diff externally |
| **Standards sharing** | One set of standards nodes, linked to requirements from all MNOs that reference them | Standards duplicated across MxN graphs |
| **Feature taxonomy sharing** | One taxonomy — "IMS Registration" feature links to VZW, TMO, and ATT requirements | Taxonomy duplicated or maintained separately per graph |
| **Ingestion isolation** | Must handle carefully — ingesting new release should not corrupt existing data | Natural isolation — each graph is independent |
| **Query routing** | One query, metadata-filtered | Must determine which graphs to query, then merge |
| **Scale** | Larger single graph (mitigated by graph DB indexing on mno+release) | Many smaller graphs (operational overhead of managing MxN stores) |

**The unified approach is superior for our use cases** because cross-MNO and cross-release queries are first-class requirements, not edge cases. The shared standards and feature nodes eliminate duplication and enable comparison queries through natural graph traversal.

**Ingestion safety:** New MNO/release ingestion is additive — new nodes and edges are added with the new `mno`+`release` metadata. Existing nodes are not modified. Standards nodes are shared but immutable once created (a 3GPP section doesn't change; if a different release of the standard is referenced, it becomes a new standards node with a different release attribute).

### 4.3 Document Organization and Folder Structure

Source documents are organized in a hierarchical folder structure that maps directly to the graph's logical partitioning:

```
/data/
├── VZW/
│   ├── 2026_Feb/
│   │   ├── Requirements/
│   │   │   ├── LTEDATARETRY.pdf
│   │   │   ├── LTESMS.pdf
│   │   │   ├── LTEAT.pdf
│   │   │   └── ...
│   │   └── TestCases/
│   │       ├── LTEDATARETRY_TC.pdf
│   │       ├── LTESMS_TC.pdf
│   │       └── ...
│   ├── 2025_Oct/
│   │   ├── Requirements/
│   │   └── TestCases/
│   └── ...
├── TMO/
│   ├── 2026_Q1/
│   │   ├── Requirements/
│   │   └── TestCases/
│   └── ...
├── ATT/
│   └── ...
└── Standards/
    ├── 3GPP/
    │   ├── TS_24.301/
    │   │   ├── Release_10/
    │   │   │   └── ts_24301_v10.pdf
    │   │   ├── Release_15/
    │   │   │   └── ts_24301_v15.pdf
    │   │   └── ...
    │   ├── TS_24.008/
    │   └── ...
    ├── GSMA/
    │   └── ...
    └── OMA/
        └── ...
```

**The folder path encodes metadata:** The ingestion pipeline derives `mno`, `release`, and `doc_type` (requirement vs. test case) from the folder path. This eliminates the need for manual metadata entry.

**Standards pre-download:** As a pre-processing step, all referenced standards specs are downloaded and organized by spec ID and release. During ingestion, the cross-reference extractor identifies which spec sections are needed, and the standards section extractor pulls them from the pre-downloaded specs.

## 5. Ingestion Pipeline

### 5.1 Document Content Extraction

**Objective:** Extract raw text, tables, images, and embedded objects from MNO requirement and test case documents in multiple formats (PDF, DOCX, XLS/XLSX), producing a **format-normalized intermediate representation** that downstream parsers consume uniformly.

#### 5.1.1 Supported Document Formats

| Format | Typical Usage | Extraction Approach |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| **PDF** | Published/finalized requirement specs, standards | `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber` |
| **DOCX** | Working/editable requirement specs, some test plans | `python-docx` for structure; `mammoth` for HTML conversion |
| **XLS/XLSX** | Requirement matrices, compliance sheets, some test cases | `openpyxl` (XLSX) / `xlrd` (XLS) or `pandas` |

487	### 5.2 Structural Parser (Requirements)
488	
489	**Objective:** Parse the normalized intermediate representation (from Section 5.1.7) into a structured requirement tree, leveraging the consistent per-MNO document format.
490	
491	**Input:** Normalized content blocks from the document content extraction layer. The parser operates on the intermediate representation regardless of original file format.
492	
493	**Approach:** Rule-based parser **specific to each MNO's document format**. A parser registry maps MNO identifiers to their corresponding parser implementation.
494	
495	```python
496	# Parser registry pattern
497	parsers = {
498	    "VZW": VZWRequirementParser(),
499	    "TMO": TMORequirementParser(),
500	    "ATT": ATTRequirementParser(),
501	}
502	```
503	
504	**VZW OA Parser** (reference implementation for PoC):
505	- **Requirement ID pattern:** `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` — globally unique, with plan name embedded
506	- **Section numbering:** Hierarchical numbering (e.g., `1.4.3.1.1.10`) up to 6+ levels deep
507	- **Plan metadata:** Plan Name, Plan ID, Version Number, Release Date in document header
508	- **Consistent section zones:**
509	  - Sections 1.1–1.2: Metadata (applicability, acronyms, hardware specs)
510	  - Section 1.3: Core software specifications (timers, algorithms, rules)
511	  - Section 1.4: Scenario-based requirements
512	
513	**Output per document:** Structured JSON with MNO and release metadata derived from folder path:

565	### 5.3 Structural Parser (Test Cases)
566	
567	**Objective:** Parse test case documents into structured test case records, linked to their corresponding requirements.

569	**Approach:** Rule-based parser specific to each MNO's test case format. Test case documents have their own format distinct from requirement documents, but are consistent within each MNO.
570	
571	**Key extraction targets:**
572	
573	| Field | Description | Purpose |
574	|-------|-------------|---------|
575	| `test_id` | Test case identifier | Unique node identity |
576	| `test_name` | Test case title | Searchable, human-readable |
577	| `description` | What the test validates | Q&A content |
578	| `preconditions` | Setup state required | Context for understanding |
579	| `steps` | Ordered test procedure | Detailed Q&A content |
580	| `expected_result` | Pass/fail criteria | Compliance evaluation |
581	| `requirement_refs` | Requirement IDs this test covers | Traceability edges |
582	| `priority` | Test priority/category | Filtering |

598	      "steps": ["..."],
599	      "expected_result": "...",

606	
607	**Traceability link extraction:** Test case documents typically reference specific requirement IDs. These references are extracted to create `tested_by` / `tests` edges in the knowledge graph. If explicit requirement ID references are absent, LLM-based matching between test case descriptions and requirement text is used as a fallback.
608	
609	### 5.4 Cross-Reference Extraction
610	
611	**Objective:** Identify all references from each requirement to other requirements, other plans (within the same MNO), and external standards.
612	
613	**Three extraction methods, in order of reliability:**
614	
615	**Method 1: Explicit Plan References (Deterministic)**
616	- Pattern match for MNO-specific requirement ID patterns (e.g., `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` for VZW)
617	- Pattern match for known plan names within the same MNO
618	- Reliability: High
619	
620	**Method 2: Standards References (Deterministic)**
621	- Regex extraction of 3GPP spec citations: `3GPP TS \d+\.\d+`, section numbers, release versions
622	- Regex for GSMA/OMA spec citations
623	- **Release resolution:** If a reference doesn't specify a standards release, use the document-level `referenced_standards_releases` mapping (from Section 5.2) to resolve the correct version
624	- Reliability: High
625	
626	**Method 3: Concept/Feature References (LLM-Assisted)**
627	- For each requirement, LLM extracts telecom concept tags (timers, procedures, interfaces, cause codes, device modes, protocols)
628	- Requirements across documents sharing concept tags are candidate cross-document links
629	- **This operates within a single MNO+release scope first**, then extends to cross-MNO concept linking via the shared feature taxonomy
630	- Reliability: Medium — requires validation
631	
632	**Output:** A cross-reference manifest per document listing all outbound references with their types.
633	
634	### 5.5 Standards Specs Ingestion (Hybrid Selective — Option C)
635	
636	**Objective:** Ingest the specific sections of 3GPP/GSMA standards that MNO requirements reference, with enough surrounding context for completeness. Handle **release-specific** standards references.
